transcript:
Interviewer: Chad, welcome to Breakpoint 2025.

Chad Cascarilla: Great to be here.

Interviewer: Awesome to have you. Um, let's get right into it. What is the latest and greatest with Paxos that we should know about, um, for an audience here that is largely sort of skeptical of the institutional adoption in, um, in the medium to long term, and a lot of developers who feel like, you know, the focus should be back to grassroots developers? So sell us on the stablecoin regulations and why this is a truly a watershed moment that's going to last longer than two years.

Chad Cascarilla: Yeah, well, um, look, I'm very sympathetic with that. Uh, I was just telling somebody, you know, Paxos has been around before Ethereum. Uh, so we're certainly, uh, in some ways OGs and very much believe in the concept and the spirit and the ethos of decentralization. And, uh, actually we just bought a wallet company called Fortify, uh, last week, which is purely for institutions who want to be able to access DeFi as an institutional self-custody wallet. Um, and I certainly believe that is really the future of the financial markets. I love how DeFi and what Solana has been able to bring to the DeFi community is, and how on-chain is going to really fundamentally change the entire basis of financial markets and I think capitalism too. Uh, all for the better. Um, at the same time, there is about $900 trillion of traditional assets, not on-chain assets, they're traditional assets, and they need to get on-chain. And in order to get them on-chain, you have to do it in some way. And actually that's why we built Paxos, which was how can we take the traditional financial world and migrate it on-chain so that it can operate in a more decentralized, open, and transparent way? And clearly the way that's happening most obviously is with dollars, which is how do you create stablecoins and have stablecoins be able to move around and be accessible to anybody. And with the passage of Genius, this is going to be done in a regulatory framework, by the way, that Paxos has followed now for seven or eight years as an issuer. And that's going to help be able to create trust and oversight and actually, um, I think a common set of standards that's very important. But it's not just about stablecoins, it's about all kinds of assets getting pushed on-chain. That's what Paxos is about. How can we use our wallet infrastructure to help create issuance and create much bigger adoption of traditional assets in an on-chain environment?

Interviewer: Okay. Uh, I think folks have heard about your, um, you know, impact on stablecoin. Uh, I want to talk a little bit more about on-chain equities and sort of the broader class of assets outside of stablecoin. Uh, but before that, uh, USDG was a new tweet that is a successful launch?

Chad Cascarilla: I think, you know, anytime you're going from zero to one, it's not easy to do. We're at about a billion and a half dollars now. And so it's been growing and the last quarter grew two and a half X. Um, I'd certainly love it to grow two and a half X every quarter. Uh, that would start to become, uh, compounding impossibly. But, uh, it has a great trajectory right now. Basically every major exchange has listed it. It's now be able, you can use it as collateral on a number of different places from OKX to Kraken, other places are, have been adding it, Bybit just joined. Um, and so it's really becoming much more ubiquitously available. It's usable in Visa and Mastercard and Stripe, a whole number of payment, uh, providers. And so I look at this and think, you know, to me, uh, there are going to be a number of rails that major, um, firms and, um, different communities are using. I think it'll be maybe two to five of them. And then there's going to be something like hundreds, maybe even thousands of white labels. And I think white labels of all kinds have a very important place to play, just like rewards cards and branded credit cards and everything. But there's still only like two to five payment networks. And I think there'll be two to five stablecoins.

Interviewer: That's part of why, sorry, sorry to interrupt you, but that was part of why I think I was really excited about USDG being sort of, once it takes off, a lot of these companies that are part of the network can start to issue white label, uh, stablecoins. Have, is there a reason we have seen a delay in that or do you think that's just, those are just conversations that take time to materialize and we'll see more of those coming out in the next year?

Chad Cascarilla: White label conversations take a while because firms, there's a bunch of different choices you can make. Do you want to kind of roll your own, the entire thing from top to bottom? Uh, do you want to use another stablecoin like USDG, uh, or the PayPal stablecoin or whatever it might be as an underlying rail? Um, uh, do you want to kind of do something like M0? Do you want to have common reserves? There's many different mechanisms you can have to create your own white label stablecoin and it also depends on what are you trying to achieve. Do you think you're going to compete as one of those two to five rails or are you doing it for your community? And you want the rewards to be able to go back to help with your token or to help with other, uh, users. And so I think there is a number of different ways you can build a white label. And once you start going down that rabbit hole, sometimes people have to spend a little bit more time than they really thought around the design phases. But ultimately you could get a stablecoin launched if you wanted to very quickly. Uh, you could do it in a couple of weeks if you wanted.

Interviewer: How long? Okay.

Chad Cascarilla: You could do it in a couple of weeks if you wanted.

Interviewer: And if you were advising say a company like Starbucks, um, on to make a choice like that, or just a company with a lot of consumers that want to have eventually a loyalty program, uh, what would, what would be the design decisions you would make on the white label?

Chad Cascarilla: I think if you're really trying to do it for your own user base, I think you're almost just creating a branding experience. If you control the UI/UX, you might not even need to have your own stablecoin. You would just control what it looks like in the UI. So that would be like the lightest version of a white label, which is we just control the UI. Guess what, you're using a Starbucks card and it's USDG underneath. And, um, then you can create whatever rewards mechanisms you want out of it. And then you could go a layer deeper, which is, you know what, it's actually, you know, S-Bucks money or something, right? And, um, and then what is the reserve assets? Do you want it to be a stablecoin that's underneath that or do you want it to be like actual treasuries? Do you want it to be Genius compliant? Uh, how do you want to make it all work? And so if I was advising someone, I would say, if you don't think it's going to be a, a global rail, and by the way, a lot of people think they're going to build global rails, a lot more than will ultimately be global rails. So there's going to be a lot of disappointing, uh, disappointing people in the world. But, um, if you don't think you're going to be a global rail, you should really piggyback off of the liquidity and the rails that other people have set up. Because it's not easy to go get yourself accepted in all the payment networks and all the exchanges and all the collateral frameworks and do all the lending and be on DeFi. It's just not going to be possible for everyone to do that. Um, so I would always try to push someone away from that, but ultimately we're an infrastructure provider. And we're neutral infrastructure. We're going to help our customers do whatever they want. Uh, who am I to say whether they're going to succeed or not? I can give them some insights from how I think the market's going to be, but, you know, I'm not here to be right or wrong about exactly where the market's going to be. What I want to be is right or wrong about the infrastructure.

Interviewer: That's awesome. Yeah, I think, uh, you know, startups can perhaps punch above their weight by, uh, investing in loyalty compared to performance marketing where a lot of the juice has been squeezed out and, you know, they're hyper efficient advertising markets. And so I was curious if you're seeing small companies also, uh, test this idea of like a, you know, white labeled stablecoin paired with a loyalty program.

Chad Cascarilla: Well, you definitely are because there's a number of companies where they have good networks, uh, remittance companies or other ones where, you know, they might even have big on-the-ground presences and they can create apps and they can create experiences. And in the case of something like USDG, one of the benefits of it is, uh, we're returning all of the rewards, all the interest is being returned. We just keep an asset management fee. And in fact, in USDG, you don't just get the interest on your own balances, you get the interest on any balances you're helping to create. Uh, now we can do that because institutions have to join. They're the distributors. Under Genius, uh, you can't pay direct rewards to the end user. So you have to do it through a distributor, um, in essence. Uh, um, and that mechanism is somewhat convoluted. I really think there should be interest-bearing stablecoins. We actually had one here out of the UAE. Um, but, you know, you're kind of having to deal with, uh, what is the art of the possible in from public policy. Uh, so I think, um, eventually you'll get to that point. Everyone's so worried about interest-bearing stablecoins, uh, but eventually you're going to have, uh, tokenized T-bills and tokenized repo and the risk-free rate is going to be democratized. The cool thing about stablecoins is they've democratized access to the dollar all over the world, but it's even, I think far more impactful when you can democratize access to the risk-free rate. And USDG does that, but maybe not totally perfectly because you can't do it directly to the end user.

Interviewer: Yeah. Um, on that note, do you think that we're moving, um, in the same direction with equities where you can democratize access to stocks and companies that are really, uh, the only instrument today to beat inflation for a lot of investors? And so democratizing access to that makes sense in the same way that we did for stablecoins, where if you have a wallet or a fintech interface, uh, you should be able to buy, uh, buy a derivative of a stock that gives you access to price action or perhaps the full stock. You know, there's a plethora of experiments. How are you involved in that? Are you involved in that? And what do you see if, uh, as Paxos, uh, you know, gets into 2026?

Chad Cascarilla: So, uh, we don't just have dollar stablecoins. We issue the PayPal, uh, dollar and the Global Dollar. We actually also issued the, uh, PAX Gold product and that is underlying gold that's held in vaults. It's about 1.4, 1.5 billion dollars as well. Uh, it's fully regulated. When you own the token, you own the gold. You can actually put in your, uh, you can come with your token and you can see the serial number of the bar that you own. Uh, it's pretty cool. It's common law, you own the bar. You don't own anything to Paxos, owe anything to Paxos.

Interviewer: And where are the vaults?

Chad Cascarilla: Uh, the vault can be anywhere. You can, it's a, it's, um, uh, you know, just issued on-chain, right? So you...

Interviewer: The, the, the gold, uh...

Chad Cascarilla: Yes.

Interviewer: Where is it custodied?

Chad Cascarilla: It's cold, the actual physical gold is custodied in London. So there's a couple different, um, uh, depositories in London, uh, ICBC and Brinks where we hold the gold. And, uh, if you own 400 ounces, you can actually come and redeem a bar. Uh, not many people do that because 400 ounces is a lot of gold. Uh, but anyways, uh, we've done that three or four times. But it's actual physical gold there. And by the way, our stablecoins are the same thing. We hold T-bills. It's not ours, it's our customers'. And I think it should be the exact same way with equities. And we actually settled real live US equity trades, uh, this is kind of forgotten in the mists of time here, but we had a no-action letter from the SEC and for two years we settled real live equity trades in a pilot form for Bank of America, Credit Suisse, SocGen, Instinet, Wedbush, a bunch of broker dealers in the US. And, uh, after Gary Gensler came in, the, uh, the no-action letter, uh, lapsed and, um, you know, kind of things went into a holding pattern. And, uh, we have now submitted an application to the SEC to be a clearing agency, which is a depository, but for stocks and for bonds. And in that way, we'll be able to hold stocks and bonds, uh, and issue them natively on-chain and you will be the holder of the stock and the bond. It won't be a derivative instrument, it will be the underlying. Just like you should be own the underlying gold, just like you should own the underlying T-bill, you should own the underlying stock. It shouldn't be a derivative instrument. I don't think there's anything wrong with that, but you really want to have leverage and counterparty risk layered on top of the underlying asset, not be the underlying asset. Uh, otherwise you're just creating too many layers of fragility. Somebody fails along the way and then you can have a problem. And, you know, you could easily have that with other instruments and that's why I think the Genius Act was important because it stopped that with stablecoins.

Interviewer: Do you think we'll get, if, with that restriction, do you think we'll get to a world where people can own stocks without KYC? Similar to how you own stablecoin with, without that?

Chad Cascarilla: I would love that to be the world. I think, uh, that's ultimately going to be up to the regulators, uh, because there's securities law restrictions on what needs to happen and what an end company needs to have. But within the extent of the, uh, within the extent securities regulation, do you think that is a realistic possibility?

Chad Cascarilla: Yes. Absolutely think it's a realistic possibility. I think, um, you're always going to end up because every jurisdiction has an issue of who is, um, an accredited investor or some version of that. And so you have to have some mechanism to gate a wallet because, uh, there's limitations on who can own certain types of securities in certain types of jurisdictions. And so taking, setting aside, uh, that layer of like compliance that's needed, it should be able to move, um, seamlessly, permissionlessly on-chain, on a public chain.

Interviewer: Yeah. I mean, the US capital markets have been the deepest capital markets for a long time now and they have a near monopoly in, um, the IPOs of some of the biggest global companies. Uh, do you see a world where, you know, we, the, you know, we actually did an analysis where we saw the volume of, uh, assets traded on different venues in the US, in China, in the long tail of regional capital markets, including the UAE, India, places like this. And if you, uh, remove Bitcoin and stablecoin volume, the internet is the third most, uh, liquid venue, uh, for trading assets. And that's all of the crypto trading that happens. So I'm curious if you think, um, in the future, in the very near future, we can partner with, you know, progressive, techno-progressive governments like the UAE and the securities sort of regulator here to have IPOs in the UAE regional market, in the, say, the Dubai Financial Market. You tokenize that on Solana and 95% of volume actually happens on Solana. And it's more volume than you would have had if this regional company had listed in the US because a lot of investors just don't know about it.

Chad Cascarilla: I, I think that's exactly what the blockchain enables. It doesn't matter where you list because you're on a public chain. And a public chain is the internet and it's open to everybody to be able to access. Whatever the restrictions are in some jurisdiction for someone to own it is a different matter. You've just created an open distribution mechanism. And, um, there's an analogy of this, which I'm unfortunately old enough to remember, which you basically had trading that was on the floor. It was manual, it was done analog with people. And you traded in like eighths and sixteenths and quarters. And, uh, the amount of volume was very low. Um, and then you went electronic and you traded decimals and everyone was so afraid, oh my god, if it goes decimals, we're not going to earn any commissions. And the amount of trading volume went up about 10 to 100 X, right? And so the amount of price compression versus the amount of trading and access that happened, uh, completely overwhelmed the compression. And I think that exact same thing will be what happens when you go on-chain. Which is you're going to completely, um, change the amount of trading that's possible and the amount of access that's possible on a global basis. So you can again 10X or 100X the amount of trading volume that happens. And there's like, um, I don't know, 250 billion dollars a day that trades in US stocks and it's really basically seven stocks or something is a majority of it, uh, or the plurality at least. And, um, you know, they're trading 250 billion dollars a day. I bet you we'll look and we'll say, wow, it trades like two and a half trillion in like, you know, some period like five or 10X, five or 10 years from here. And we'll all be surprised and, uh, everyone would have been so afraid of it going on-chain and it actually was the best thing for capital markets and for liquidity and for companies to raise money.

Interviewer: Yeah, that's awesome. You betrayed your age with that example. Uh, but, um, I agree. And I think, uh, the analogy I like to use is like, you know, you were watching an HBO show, I don't know how many views you got, but now, but you know, if you're outside the US, it's hard to get access to HBO. Your cable operator needs to probably have a partnership with HBO to get it. But then if you have a YouTube link, you can click on that and anybody anywhere in the world can watch the same video. And similarly, any asset listed, hopefully by Paxos, uh, that's a reflection of the traditional finance financial market can be purchased by anybody anywhere around the world, allowing them to create a portfolio that's resistant to all of the, you know, uncertainty and inflation that, uh, we, uh, we're likely to face. So that's, uh, that's really exciting. We're very glad to work with you and thank you for joining us.

Chad Cascarilla: Great to be here. Thanks for having me.

Interviewer: Thank you very much.

Chad Cascarilla: All right, thanks.

Today marks an important milestone for Stable and for the broader stablecoin economy. Stable Mainnet is officially live, activating StableChain, our USDT-native Layer 1 designed for high-volume, predictable stablecoin settlement.
This moment represents more than a technical release: it is the beginning of dedicated infrastructure for the stablecoin economy. As we move into this next phase, we want to outline the thesis that guides our work and the role StableChain will play in shaping the future of digital value movement.
Learn more about StableChain:[www.stable.xyz](http://www.stable.xyz)
## **Stable Thesis, Why Stable?**
With Stable Mainnet going live today, we are entering an important moment for both Stable and the broader stablecoin economy. Mainnet marks the point where dedicated stablecoin infrastructure moves from concept to reality, and with it, the opportunity to redefine how stable value moves across the world.
Stablecoins have become one of the world’s most widely adopted digital assets, yet the infrastructure that carries them has not evolved at the pace of their usage. They are used globally, across industries and economies, but they still rely on base layers built for purposes other than payments.
This gap is no longer sustainable.
Stable’s mission is to deliver dedicated payment infrastructure designed around how stablecoins are actually used: to move value quickly, reliably, and with predictable costs. With StableChain, we are advancing that mission: providing a foundation where stablecoin transactions are not secondary activity, but the core function of the system.
Stable Mainnet represents the beginning of this new phase.
**Fast, Secure, Predictable.**
## **Growing Ecosystem of Day-1 Partners**

sources.title: Paxos Labs debuts USDG0 to extend its regulated stablecoin across multiple blockchains
sources.snippet:
[Nate Kostar](https://cointelegraph.com/authors/nate-kostar)

21 hours ago

# Paxos Labs debuts USDG0 to extend its regulated stablecoin across multiple blockchains

The new omnichain token brings fully backed dollar liquidity to Hyperliquid, Plume and Aptos while keeping a single regulated supply across networks.

Listen 0:00

2134

News

COINTELEGRAPH IN YOUR SOCIAL FEED

Paxos Labs has launched USDG0, an omnichain extension of its regulated USDG stablecoin, bringing fully backed dollar liquidity to Hyperliquid, Plume and Aptos through LayerZero’s OFT standard.

According to an X post from Paxos Labs on Tuesday, USDG0 extends USDG ( [USDG](https://cointelegraph.com/global-dollar-price-index)), a 1:1 dollar-backed stablecoin issued by Paxos and governed by the Global Dollar Network, to new chains without creating separate wrapped versions.

By using LayerZero’s OFT standard, USDG0 can move across blockchains as a single native asset while preserving the same regulatory protections and backing as USDG on Ethereum, Solana, Ink and X Layer.

_Source:_ [_Paxos Labs_](https://x.com/paxoslabs/status/1990786568597221728?s=20)

Paxos Labs said the initial rollout showcases how different networks can plug into the stablecoin’s economics. On Hyperliquid, USDG0 will support yield-aligned trading and new lending markets, while Plume and Aptos plan to use it to power modular DeFi, tokenized yields and enterprise-grade stablecoin rails.

Across all three ecosystems, USDG0 is designed to enable apps to embed dollar liquidity into their products, earn yield tied to Treasury benchmarks, and transfer value between chains without relying on traditional bridges.

The company said the initiative represents “how regulated infrastructure meets the composability of DeFi and how trusted money becomes truly borderless.”

Since 2018, Paxos has processed more than $180 billion in tokenization activity under the oversight of global regulators. The company oversees three regulated dollar-backed stablecoins: USDP, PayPal’s PYUSD and USDG.

_**Related:**_ [_**Visa pilots fiat-funded stablecoin payouts for US businesses**_](https://cointelegraph.com/news/visa-stablecoin-fiat-funded-payout-pilot)

## Stablecoins around the world

Regulatory clarity in the United States under the [GENIUS Act](https://cointelegraph.com/news/stablecoin-genius-act-donald-trump-signing) and in Europe through the Markets in Crypto-Assets ( [MiCA](https://cointelegraph.com/learn/articles/markets-in-crypto-assets-regulation-mica)) framework has helped drive a surge in stablecoin adoption. According to DefiLlama [data](https://defillama.com/stablecoins), the stablecoin market cap stands at $303.44 billion, up nearly $100 billion since the start of the year.

While the stablecoin market remains dominated by Tether’s USDt ( [USDT](https://cointelegraph.com/tether-price-index)) and Circle’s USDC ( [USDC](https://cointelegraph.com/usdc-price-index)), several other players have entered the market this year from all around the world.

_Stablecoin Market Cap. Source:_ [_Defillama_](https://defillama.com/stablecoins)

In October, Western Union [announced](https://ir.westernunion.com/news/archived-press-releases/press-release-details/2025/Western-Union-Announces-USDPT-Stablecoin-on-Solana-and-Digital-Asset-Network/default.aspx) plans to launch USDPT, a US dollar-pegged stablecoin issued by Anchorage Digital Bank on Solana. The token is designed to connect the company’s digital and fiat payment rails and support its global money-movement and treasury operations.

The same month, JPYC, a Tokyo-based fintech company, launched [Japan’s first yen-backed stablecoin](https://cointelegraph.com/news/yen-backed-stablecoin-launches-in-japan), a 1:1 yen-pegged token supported by bank deposits and government bonds.

In Europe, a consortium of nine banks announced in September that they’ll launch [a stablecoin pegged to the euro](https://cointelegraph.com/news/unicredit-ing-nine-banks-euro-stablecoin-mica),

tags.impact_reason: Paxos' discussion of on-chain assets and stablecoin regulation could influence institutional adoption on Solana, but the immediate impact seems moderate.

The vault is set into a vast, dark chamber, with the deep background showing rows of server racks and data conduits fading into the atmospheric haze, pierced by sharp volumetric light beams.

Just in front of this colossal door, a crisp, holographic projection shimmers and floats in the atmospheric haze, displaying the text '$900T'. The vault is grandly set into a vast, dark chamber, illuminated by dramatic high-contrast cinematic lighting. In the deep background, rows of server racks and intricate data conduits recede into a thick atmospheric haze, dramatically pierced by sharp, directional volumetric light beams. The composition emphasizes epic height and scale, with ample negative space above the vault, making it feel truly monumental. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, physically based rendering (PBR), cinematic lighting, volumetric lighting, holographic glow, 3:4 aspect ratio.

editorial.headline: Paxos Files SEC Application to Become Clearing Agency for Native On-Chain Equities
editorial.standfirst: Moving beyond stablecoins, Paxos seeks to tokenize the $900 trillion traditional asset market by holding underlying securities directly on-chain. The move follows the firm's $1.5 billion USDG growth and the recent passage of the Genius Act.
key_takeaways: Paxos has officially filed an application with the SEC to become a fully registered Clearing Agency for stocks and bonds.
The **Global Dollar (USDG)** has reached $1.5 billion in market cap, growing 2.5x last quarter with adoption from **OKX**, **Kraken**, and **Stripe**.
CEO Chad Cascarilla predicts the stablecoin market will consolidate into "two to five" major rails that support thousands of white-label issuers.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
In a move that signals the next phase of institutional crypto adoption, **Paxos** has filed an application with the **SEC** to become a registered Clearing Agency. Announced at Breakpoint 2025, this filing aims to allow the firm to issue stocks and bonds natively on-chain. Unlike current tokenized equity models that rely on derivatives or special purpose vehicles, a clearing agency license would enable **Paxos** to custody the underlying securities directly. This distinction is critical for institutional risk management, removing layers of counterparty fragility and enabling true ownership of the asset rather than a synthetic claim.

The timing of the application capitalizes on the regulatory clarity provided by the **Genius Act**, signed into law in July 2025. With a federal framework for stablecoins now established, **Paxos** is pivoting its focus to the broader $900 trillion market of traditional assets. CEO Chad Cascarilla argues that the democratization of access seen with the US Dollar via stablecoins is the blueprint for global capital markets. He envisions a future where public blockchains serve as the third-largest liquidity venue globally, granting international investors permissionless access to US-listed equities without the friction of traditional brokerage borders.

Meanwhile, **Paxos**'s flagship yield-bearing asset, **USDG**, has surged to $1.5 billion in market cap, growing 2.5x in the last quarter alone. The growth is driven by a B2B distribution model where yield is shared with partners like **OKX**, **Kraken**, and **Robinhood** rather than end-users—a compliance necessity under the new Genius Act regulations. While Cascarilla foresees thousands of white-label stablecoins launching for loyalty and community purposes, he asserts that liquidity will fragment across only "two to five" dominant global rails, positioning **USDG** to be a foundational layer for this consolidated infrastructure.
